metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Choral Music 12 is the capstone choral course, developing students as accomplished singers, musical leaders, and reflective artists. Students perform challenging repertoire across diverse traditions, take on leadership roles within the ensemble, and deepen their understanding of choral artistry and the cultural significance of choral music. The course cultivates lifelong musical participation and prepares students for post-secondary choral music, music education, or continued community singing. Senior students serve as musical models and mentors within the ensemble.
metadata.general_description: The capstone choral course developing vocal mastery, ensemble leadership, advanced repertoire study, and critical musicianship — building lifelong choral artistry and musical leadership.

big_ideas.statement: Mastery in the arts involves sustained practice, critical reflection, and willingness to take creative risks.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduating students understand that artistic excellence is achieved through disciplined practice, honest self-assessment, and the courage to attempt challenging, expressive work.

big_ideas.statement: Artists contribute to their communities by sharing their work and developing the next generation of artists.
big_ideas.elaboration: Senior students embrace leadership roles — mentoring younger singers, contributing to ensemble culture, and sharing their musical gifts with the broader community.

big_ideas.statement: Choral music is a global language that builds connection across cultures and generations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage with the full breadth of the choral tradition — across centuries, cultures, and contexts — understanding choral music as a living art form that connects humanity.

big_ideas.statement: Exploring stories and other texts helps us understand ourselves and make connections to others and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through the study and performance of diverse choral texts, senior students deepen their capacity for empathy, cross-cultural understanding, and personal meaning-making.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate mastery of vocal technique and ensemble skills across challenging repertoire; take on leadership roles in ensemble rehearsal and performance; demonstrate advanced music literacy and score reading; contribute to ensemble artistic vision and interpretation.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate mastery of vocal technique — tone, breath, intonation, diction, and range — across challenging repertoire.
I can take on section leadership, vocal modelling, and rehearsal facilitation roles within the ensemble.
I can read and interpret complex choral scores with accuracy and musical insight.
I can contribute to the ensemble's artistic interpretation — suggesting phrasings, expressive choices, and musical nuance.
I can perform with consistent artistic commitment, communicating the emotional and cultural meaning of choral texts.

competencies.raw: Critically evaluate complex choral performances using sophisticated musical vocabulary; analyze the relationship between choral repertoire, cultural context, and artistic intent; reflect on personal artistic legacy and future musical participation.
i_can_statements: I can conduct sophisticated critical analysis of choral performances — my own and professional recordings — using precise musical vocabulary.
I can articulate the relationship between a choral work's cultural origins, compositional choices, and artistic intent.
I can reflect on my full journey as a choral singer and articulate my plans for continued musical participation.
I can mentor peers in developing their critical listening and reflective practice skills.

areas.name: Advanced Vocal Technique
areas.raw: Mastery of vocal production, stylistic flexibility, and vocal health.
topics: Advanced resonance and tone: developing a rich, consistent tone across the full range
Stylistic flexibility: adapting vocal technique to Early Music, contemporary, world, and popular styles
Advanced diction: Latin, German, French, Italian, and world language pronunciation in choral contexts
Vocal agility: runs, melisma, and ornament technique in Baroque and other repertoire
Extended vocal techniques: exploring contemporary choral effects with artistic purpose
Long-term vocal health: sustainable practice habits for a lifetime of singing

areas.name: Musical Leadership
areas.raw: Ensemble leadership, section leadership, and choral mentorship.
topics: Section leadership: modelling, supporting, and developing section tone and blend
Rehearsal facilitation: leading warm-ups, sectionals, and peer rehearsal strategies
Score study and preparation: independently preparing parts and supporting ensemble learning
Mentoring younger singers: developing patient, effective peer teaching skills
Conducting fundamentals: basic conducting patterns and rehearsal communication
Program planning: contributing ideas for repertoire selection and concert programming

areas.name: Advanced Repertoire and Historical Study
areas.raw: Challenging choral repertoire spanning history, culture, and genre.
topics: Major choral works: large-scale pieces (oratorio, mass, cantata, or contemporary equivalent)
Choral music history: from Gregorian chant through contemporary Canadian and global works
First Peoples choral traditions: advanced engagement with Indigenous vocal music and protocols
Global choral music: African, Latin, Asian, and world traditions at an advanced level
Contemporary Canadian choral music: commissioning, premiering, and celebrating Canadian composers
Choral music in social context: music's role in social movements, celebrations, and healing

areas.name: Advanced Music Literacy and Theory
areas.raw: Advanced score reading, harmonic understanding, and analytical musicianship.
topics: Advanced sight-reading: reading chromatic passages, complex rhythms, and modern notation
Harmonic awareness: understanding chord function, voice leading, and harmonic tension/resolution in choral singing
Form and analysis: analyzing large-scale choral structures and compositional techniques
Transposition and key relationships: understanding tonal relationships in performance
Choral arranging basics: creating simple arrangements for ensemble voices
Critical listening: analysis of contrasting recordings and performances of the same work

areas.name: Capstone Performance and Legacy
areas.raw: Senior performance leadership, concert presentation, and artistic legacy.
topics: Capstone concert performance: senior leadership in the year's major concert events
Solo performance: preparing and presenting a solo choral or art song excerpt
Festival and competition: representing the ensemble at adjudicated choral events
Recording and documentation: archiving the ensemble's work and contributing to its legacy
Post-secondary choral pathways: university choirs, community choral societies, and audition preparation
Personal artistic statement: reflecting on musical identity and commitment to lifelong choral participation
